passages.sentenceTheme: Confidence in the LORD’s shepherding care, provision, guidance, protection, and faithful love throughout life and beyond.
David proclaims fearless trust in the LORD, longs to dwell in His presence, seeks His face, and waits confidently for His deliverance amid enemies.
David praises the LORD for deliverance and teaches the fear of God, assuring the righteous of His protection and justice.
A downcast soul thirsts for God and finds hope in His steadfast love despite oppression.
God is our unshakable refuge and ever-present help, who stills chaos, protects His city, and is exalted among the nations.
David pleads for God’s mercy, cleansing, and renewal of heart and spirit after sin, vowing to teach others and worship rightly.
David laments oppression and the betrayal of a close friend, crying to God for deliverance and placing his trust in the LORD.
David cries to God from distress, seeks refuge and guidance, and vows lifelong praise for God’s preserving mercy and truth.
Trust in God alone with quiet confidence; He is our rock, salvation, and refuge, and to Him belong power and mercy as He repays each according to their work.
God protects and delivers those who dwell in Him, trust Him, and call upon His name.
David praises the LORD for His forgiving, healing, compassionate, and everlasting covenant mercy that rules over all and calls all creation to bless His name.
The LORD, vigilant and unfailing, guards His people day and night, providing steadfast help and preservation in all their ways.
God’s intimate knowledge, ever-present nearness, and holy guidance surround and shape the psalmist’s life.
David pleads for God's mercy, guidance, and deliverance, trusting in His righteousness.
David declares the LORD as his refuge and portion, rejoices in His counsel and presence, and trusts that God will not abandon him to the grave but will lead him to life and fullness of joy.
David praises the LORD for powerful deliverance, protection, and vindication, recounting how God rescued him and established him in victory.
David praises the LORD for deliverance, commits to faithful obedience, proclaims God’s righteousness, and pleads for help amid overwhelming troubles.
Amid relentless enemies, David trusts God, confident that the Lord notes his tears and will deliver him into the light of life.
Asaph struggles with the prosperity of the wicked until entering God’s sanctuary, where he understands their end and reaffirms God as his portion and refuge.
Moses contrasts God’s eternity with human frailty and pleads for mercy, wisdom, joy, and the establishment of our work.

The psalmist closes with a heartfelt prayer for understanding, deliverance, and guidance, expressing devotion to God's commandments while humbly acknowledging his need for God to seek and restore him.
God creates the heavens and the earth in six days, forming and filling creation and blessing life.

The LORD calls Abram to leave his homeland with promises of blessing and land; Abram journeys to Canaan, builds altars to the LORD, and later goes to Egypt where Sarai is taken by Pharaoh and then restored.
God tests Abraham by commanding him to offer Isaac, then provides a ram and reaffirms His covenant promises.
Joseph, favored by Jacob, dreams of preeminence, inciting his brothers’ envy, who sell him into slavery while deceiving their father.
Joseph prospers under the LORD’s presence, resists his master’s wife, is falsely accused, and is shown favor even in prison.
Joseph interprets Pharaoh's dreams and is elevated to rule Egypt, preparing during abundance for the coming years of famine.
Joseph reveals himself to his brothers, explains God’s providence in sending him ahead to preserve life, and invites Jacob’s household to settle in Egypt.
Joseph buries Jacob in Canaan, reassures and forgives his brothers, and dies in Egypt after prophesying Israel’s future deliverance.
God reveals Himself to Moses at the burning bush, declares His name I AM, and commissions Moses to deliver Israel from Egypt.
The LORD parts the sea to deliver Israel and destroys the pursuing Egyptians.
God provides manna and quail for Israel, commands daily gathering with a double portion on the sixth day, institutes Sabbath rest, and preserves an omer as a memorial.
Twelve leaders scout Canaan; most bring a fearful report, but Caleb urges faith to possess the land.
Moses encourages Israel, commissions Joshua, commands the public reading of the law, and delivers a prophetic song as a witness of Israel’s future apostasy.
God commissions Joshua to lead Israel with strength and obedience, promising His presence and success as they prepare to enter the promised land.
Amid famine and loss, Naomi returns from Moab, and Ruth commits herself to Naomi and to the LORD.
Ruth humbly gleans in Boaz’s field, receiving generous favor and protection, revealing God’s providential care for Naomi’s family.
In sorrow Hannah prays and vows her son to the LORD; God remembers her, Samuel is born, and she dedicates him at Shiloh.
God sends Samuel to anoint David as king, and David’s Spirit-empowered musicianship soothes Saul’s troubled spirit.
David, trusting in the Lord, defeats Goliath, demonstrating that victory comes not by human strength but by faith in God.
David, seeking the LORD, pursues the Amalekites, recovers all that was taken at Ziklag, establishes a just sharing statute, and sends gifts to Judah’s elders.
Nathan confronts David’s sin; God pronounces judgment; David repents, the child dies, Solomon is born, and David leads Israel to capture Rabbah.
Elijah flees Jezebel, is sustained by an angel, meets God at Horeb in a gentle voice, and receives a renewed commission to anoint future leaders and call Elisha as his successor.
Through Elisha, God miraculously rescues, reveals, and provides amid conflict and famine.
Nehemiah receives royal approval and resources, surveys Jerusalem’s ruins, and rallies the people to rebuild despite opposition.
Amid mockery and threats, the people pray, prepare defenses, and continue building the wall with vigilance and resolve.
Mordecai urges Esther to intercede for her people, leading to a communal fast and her resolve to risk her life before the king.
Satan challenges Job’s integrity before the LORD, and Job loses his wealth, servants, and children, yet responds with humble worship and without sin.
Job curses the day of his birth and laments his suffering, longing for rest in death.
Job laments deep abandonment and injustice, yet declares unwavering confidence that his living Redeemer will ultimately vindicate him.
The LORD challenges Job from the whirlwind with probing questions that reveal His unrivaled wisdom and sovereign rule over creation.
Job humbly repents, God rebukes his friends, and the LORD restores and blesses Job with renewed prosperity and family.
Trust in the LORD, prize wisdom above wealth, and live righteously with generosity, humility, and integrity for God’s guidance and blessing.
A father urges his son to prize wisdom, walk the right path, and avoid the way of the wicked.
Contrasting the ways and outcomes of the righteous and the wicked, this chapter urges wise, truthful, diligent living under the LORD’s favor.
God ordains every season and time, calling people to rejoice in His gifts, fear Him, and recognize His sovereign work amid life’s cycles and judgments.
God announces comfort for His people, reveals His unrivaled greatness as Creator, and promises renewed strength to those who wait on Him.
The LORD reassures Israel with His presence and strength, promising deliverance and exposing idols as powerless.
God reassures Israel of His redemptive presence, protection, and purpose, calling them to witness His uniqueness and to return from weariness and sin to His forgiving grace.
The Servant is despised and afflicted, bears our sins, and through his sacrificial suffering brings justification to many.
God freely invites all to come, promising sure covenant mercies, powerful and effectual word, and a joyful, peaceful restoration.
Jeremiah’s letter instructs the exiles to seek the peace of Babylon, promises return after seventy years, and condemns false prophets.
In the depths of suffering, the poet remembers the LORD’s steadfast mercies and finds hope, urging humble repentance and trust in God’s just compassion.
Daniel and his friends resolve not to defile themselves with the king's food, and God grants them favor and wisdom before the king.
Nebuchadnezzar’s image is defied by Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego, who are delivered by God from the fiery furnace.
Daniel’s unwavering faith leads to deliverance from the lions’ den and a royal proclamation honoring the living God.
Israel is called to return to the LORD, who promises healing, renewal, and righteous paths for the wise.
From the fish’s belly Jonah prays to the LORD, who delivers him.
The LORD brings a covenant lawsuit against His people, calling them to justice, mercy, and humble obedience, and warning of desolation for persistent wickedness.
Habakkuk offers a prayer-psalm recalling God’s awesome coming, pleading for mercy amid wrath, and resolving to rejoice and trust in the Lord’s saving strength.
Jesus proclaims the Beatitudes and intensifies the Law’s intent, calling his disciples to wholehearted righteousness, integrity, and enemy-love.
Jesus teaches sincere devotion to the Father through giving, prayer, and fasting, warns against hypocrisy and anxiety, and calls disciples to seek God's kingdom and righteousness first.
Jesus answers John’s doubts, rebukes unrepentant cities, and invites the weary to find rest in Him.
John is beheaded; Jesus feeds five thousand and walks on the sea, calling His disciples to faith.
Jesus teaches the crowds parables about the kingdom and later calms a great storm on the sea.
Jesus delivers the Gerasene demoniac, heals a woman with an issue of blood, and raises Jairus's daughter, revealing his power and calling for faith.
Jesus sends the seventy, warns unrepentant cities, rejoices in the Father’s revelation, and teaches love through the Good Samaritan and devotion through Mary and Martha.
Jesus teaches, through the parables of the lost sheep, coin, and son, that heaven rejoices over repentant sinners and the Father’s grace eagerly restores the lost.

Religious leaders plot against Jesus, Judas betrays Him, the Passover and the Lord’s Supper are observed, Jesus prays on the Mount of Olives, is arrested, Peter denies Him, and He is examined before the council.
The eternal Word became flesh, bringing light and life, as John bears witness and the first disciples begin to follow Jesus.
Jesus raises Lazarus and reveals himself as the resurrection and the life, leading many to believe while the leaders plot against him.
Jesus comforts His disciples, reveals His unity with the Father, promises the Comforter, and calls for believing, loving obedience.
Jesus teaches that abiding in Him produces lasting fruit and loving obedience amid the world’s hatred, and He promises the Spirit’s testimony.
Jesus warns of coming persecution, promises the Comforter who will convict the world and guide the disciples into all truth, and assures them of joy and peace despite tribulation.
Jesus rises from the dead, appears to His disciples, commissions them with peace and the Spirit, and calls them to believe for life in His name.

The risen Jesus appears by the Sea of Tiberias, provides a miraculous catch, restores Peter with a threefold charge to tend His flock, and foretells Peter’s future while correcting a rumor about the beloved disciple.
Saul encounters the risen Jesus, is converted and commissioned, while Peter heals Aeneas and raises Dorcas, and the church grows in the fear of the Lord and comfort of the Holy Spirit.
Paul and Silas, guided by the Holy Spirit, bring the gospel to Macedonia, witness Lydia’s and the jailer’s conversions, and are vindicated after imprisonment in Philippi.
Through Christ, God justifies sinners by faith, granting peace, hope, and abundant grace that reigns unto eternal life.
Life in the Spirit through Christ frees believers from condemnation and empowers them for holiness, hope, and unshakable love.
Believers, transformed by renewed minds, humbly serve in love and overcome evil with good.
Paul exalts love above all gifts, describing its character and permanence until perfection comes.
Paul blesses God for comfort in afflictions, shares how suffering produces consolation and deliverance, defends his integrity in travel plans, and points to God’s faithfulness and the Spirit’s seal.
Paul proclaims the gospel with integrity, endures suffering, and fixes hope on the unseen, eternal glory revealed in Christ.

Paul recounts surpassing visions, explains his thorn given to keep him humble, boasts only in weaknesses where Christ’s grace is sufficient, expresses selfless pastoral care, and warns the Corinthians against ongoing sins.
Paul urges believers to gently restore the fallen, share burdens, sow to the Spirit, persevere in doing good, and boast only in the cross of Christ.
God makes the formerly dead alive with Christ by grace through faith, uniting Jew and Gentile into one new humanity and building them into God’s dwelling by the Spirit.
Paul explains the revealed mystery that Gentiles are fellow-heirs in Christ, prays for the believers’ inner strengthening and love, and ends with doxology.
Household obedience and servant leadership culminate in the call to stand firm in God’s armour through prayer and steadfast spiritual warfare.
Paul rejoices in the Philippians’ partnership in the gospel, confident that God will complete His work in them, and urges steadfast, Christ-honoring conduct amid opposition.
Christ's humility and exaltation model unity and obedient service.

Paul exhorts the Philippians to rejoice always, pray with gratitude, think on what is good, practice what they’ve learned, and find contentment through Christ who strengthens, trusting God’s peace and provision.
Raised with Christ, believers must set their minds above, put to death sinful ways, put on Christlike virtues of love and peace, and honor the Lord in every word, home relationship, and work.
Live alert, holy, and thankful lives as we await the Lord’s coming, encouraging one another in faith, love, and peace.
Paul urges Timothy to guard the gospel without shame, endure hardship, and rely on the Holy Spirit’s power.
Believers are urged to enter God's promised rest through faith, while holding fast to our confession under our great High Priest, Jesus.
Hebrews 11 recounts how men and women trusted God’s promises and acted by faith, obtaining witness from creation through the patriarchs and prophets.
Believers are called to run with endurance under the Lord’s loving discipline, heed His speaking, and receive the unshakable kingdom with reverent service.
Endure trials with faith, ask God for wisdom, be doers of the word, and practice pure, undefiled religion.
James urges the rich to repent, calls believers to patient endurance until the Lord’s coming, and emphasizes honest speech, prayer, confession, and restoring the erring.
God’s great mercy gives believers a living hope that refines faith through trials and calls them to holy living.
Peter exhorts church elders to shepherd humbly, calls all believers to humility and vigilance, and assures God’s restoring grace amid suffering.
Believers discern spirits and abide in God, whose perfect love drives out fear.
God unveils a new heaven and earth and the holy city, the New Jerusalem, where He dwells with His people forever.
